contest.title: Codeforces Round 1092 (Unrated, Div. 1, Based on THUPC 2026 Finals)

sources.notes: Statement and constraints: alternating card game with one known green card position and huge n,k,s.
Official editorial and reference implementation used for the minimax recurrence, residue cases, and O(sqrt n) jump optimization.

reference_candidates.notes: Candidate Luogu solution page; not used as evidence in this review.

annotation.summary: Alice and Bob alternately expose up to k top cards from a deck containing one known green card. Alice wants to discard it herself as late as possible for high score; Bob wants score 0 or as small as possible.
annotation.constraints: n, k, s <= 1e9, so turn simulation is impossible; the solution needs a compressed game state and jump recurrence.
annotation.core_idea: Reduce to f_k(n), the optimal score when Alice is about to take the green card at the top. Returning the green card is useful except at residue n == k mod 2k, where Alice cannot force the next capture and should score 1. Otherwise, after x=floor((n+k)/(2k)) rounds the game returns to a similar top-card form with pile size near n-x; delete-or-not choices form a local minimax over n-x-1, n-x, n-x+1. A local-peak property leaves only a few exceptional residue cases, and stretches with the same x can be jumped in batches, yielding O(sqrt n) states.
annotation.complexity: O(sqrt n) memoized states per test case.
tricks: reduce arbitrary green-card position to the top-card game
residue classes modulo 2k decide forced scoring cases
batch jump ordinary recurrence steps with the same x

solution_variants.name: residue-minimax-dp
solution_variants.summary: Memoize f_k(n) for the top-card state, handle exceptional n mod 2k cases, and batch-jump repeated f(n)=f(n-x)+1 transitions while x=floor((n+k)/(2k)) is unchanged.

tags.evidence: The core is an optimal-play minimax game between Alice's score-maximizing choices and Bob's score-minimizing choices.
The solution defines and memoizes f_k(n), then derives transitions between smaller pile sizes instead of simulating turns.
The decisive cases are residues modulo 2k.
The implementation jumps many identical ordinary recurrence steps while x=floor((n+k)/(2k)) remains unchanged.
A local-peak property of neighboring f values is used to collapse the general minimax into a small recurrence.
